subject.title: English Lit (Old)
subject.description: IB English Literature focuses exclusively on the study of literature, developing students' appreciation and critical analysis of literary works from various cultures, periods, and genres. Students examine poetry, prose, and drama through close reading, comparative analysis, and exploration of literary techniques and themes. The course cultivates sophisticated interpretive skills and deep understanding of how literature reflects and shapes human experience across different cultural contexts.

strengths: The title and opening communicate a comparative focus on nineteenth-century versus contemporary diction.
Some link between the research question and discussion is maintained, indicating at least a basic investigative intent.
weaknesses: The research question is grammatically faulty and conceptually vague, lacking precise parameters.
Methodology is not justified or detailed, with no clear criteria for text selection or analytic tools.
Focus drifts intermittently and explanations are repetitive and confusing.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall performance in Focus and Method sits at the lower end of the 3â4 band and upper end of the 1â2 band. While the topic and comparative intent are communicated, the research question is unclear and the methodology is virtually absent. A score of 2 reflects that some focus exists but remains underdeveloped and unsupported.

strengths: The student demonstrates a basic overview of Romantic-era norms and how they might influence diction.
Some coherent character analysis shows an attempt to link diction to societal expectations.
weaknesses: Knowledge of sources remains surface-level and descriptive, with no engagement of secondary criticism.
Terminology is often misused (e.g., âaddictionâ for âdictionâ) and applied inconsistently.
Several factual errors and character name confusions undermine credibility.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Knowledge and Understanding is firmly within the 1â2 band but at the very bottom due to misapplied terminology and lack of scholarly depth. A single mark reflects minimal but present content knowledge that fails to engage effectively with sources or concepts.

strengths: Insightful connection drawn between social media influence and colloquial diction in modern romance.
Thematic comparison (emotional restraint vs. expression) shows some conceptual depth.
weaknesses: Research is limited to a small set of primary texts with no scholarly or methodological sources.
Analysis is largely descriptive with unsupported generalizations and no systematic textual evidence.
Discussion lacks critical evaluation, consideration of limitations, or alternative viewpoints.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Critical Thinking performance falls within the 1â3 band. While there are occasional thematic insights, the work overall is characterized by superficial analysis, limited relevance of sources, and an absence of critical evaluation. A score of 2 acknowledges these few strengths but reflects predominantly underdeveloped critical thinking.

strengths: The report includes all major structural elements (title page, contents, headings, works cited).
A word count and table of contents are provided, indicating some formal compliance.
weaknesses: Citation style is inconsistent and unreliable; no secondary sources are included in the bibliography.
Abstract is missing and section headings are occasionally misaligned or mislabeled.
Page numbers are absent or inconsistently applied; multiple spelling and formatting errors distract the reader.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation meets only the basic criteria for structure and layout (1â2 band), but numerous formatting inconsistencies and incomplete elements prevent higher credit. A score of 1 reflects minimal adherence to presentation standards.

strandSelections.reasoning: Methodology is limited to selecting six primary novels without explaining criteria for selection, analytical tools, or secondary scholarship. There is no evidence of a systematic approach (e.g., lexical statistics, close-reading protocol). Source range is narrow and almost entirely primary. This meets only the minimum characteristics of band 1-2, and the lower mark (1) is warranted because informed selection is virtually absent.

Basic formatting conventions are observed (word count stated, table of contents provided), yet citation style is inconsistent, images are unexplained, and page numbers are not visible in the extract. These issues indicate that only some layout considerations are met, situating the work at the bottom of band 1-2 with 1 mark.

Research is confined to six primary texts; the Works Cited lists no scholarly criticism, linguistics sources, or historical studies. While the novels are relevant to the question, overall research depth and breadth are minimal, aligning with the middle of the 1-3 band.

The title and opening paragraphs communicate that the essay will compare diction in nineteenth-century and contemporary romance fiction. Although phrasing is clumsy, the reader can understand the object of study and the general purpose. The focus is not sustained with precision, but the topic itself is conveyed with âadequateâ rather than âlimitedâ clarity, placing it most naturally in the 3-4 band; the lower value in the band (3) is appropriate because explanations are repetitive and occasionally confusing.

The discussion strings together thematic parallels but lacks critical evaluation; opposing viewpoints, limitations, or methodological reflection are missing. Argument progression is weak and repetitive, characteristic of the bottom of band 1-3, so 1 is awarded.

Analysis largely retells plots and asserts general trends (âlanguage is more casual nowâ) without systematic textual evidence. Occasional quotations or specific word choices are absent. Conclusions about evolution of diction are asserted rather than demonstrated, so analysis is âlimited,â meriting 2 within the 1-3 range.

The essay contains all required structural elements: title page, contents, section headings, conclusion, and works cited. Sequencing is logical, though headings occasionally mislabel content. Minor omissions (no abstract, some duplication) prevent the highest mark, but overall structure is âclearly appropriate,â justifying the lower value (3) in the 3-4 band.

The student demonstrates basic familiarity with plot lines and broad historical context of Austen and three recent romance novels, but knowledge remains surface-level and largely descriptive. No secondary criticism is engaged, and several factual errors and confusions appear. This is characteristic of âlimited knowledge,â yet some correct content is present, so the top of band 1-2 (2) is appropriate.

Literary terms such as âdiction,â âtone,â and âromanticismâ are mentioned, but they are often misapplied (e.g., âaddictionâ for âdictionâ) and not used analytically. Subject-specific vocabulary is inconsistent and sometimes incorrect, fitting the descriptor of âunclear and limitedâ usage at the lower end of band 1-2.

The research question â âTo what extent are the similar correlations of dictionâ¦?â â is grammatically faulty and conceptually vague. It indicates comparison but offers no clear parameters (correlations of what features? which texts? how measured?). The discussion refers only intermittently to the question, so the focus is weak. Because a question is at least stated and loosely connected to the essay, the strand reaches band 1-2; the top of that band (2) reflects that some link exists but remains blurred.

annotations.comment: The title effectively communicates the comparative scope between Romantic and 21st-century diction, establishing a clear focus for the investigation.
The research question is grammatically faulty and conceptually vague; specify key terms (e.g., âdictionâ) and define parameters to sharpen its focus.
Word count is clearly stated and falls within the 4,000-word limit, satisfying the formatting requirement.
The table of contents lists all sections logically, but consider standardizing section titles and page-number formatting for consistency.
The introduction misuses âaddictionâ instead of âdiction,â which undermines clarity; ensure correct terminology to maintain precision.
Contextual background is largely descriptive; integrate scholarly secondary sources to support historical claims and deepen your analysis.
The rationale section remains descriptive and omits methodological reflection; discuss your selection criteria and analytical approach explicitly.
Revise the research question to specify the corpus, variables, and analytical methodsâfor example, define how you will measure âdictionâ across texts.
This paragraph offers a solid overview of Romantic-era social norms and their impact on diction, demonstrating clear understanding.
Multiple spelling errors (e.g., âAustinâ inconsistently spelled) and unclear phrasing distract the reader; proofread rigorously.
Include direct quotations from Austenâs texts to substantiate claims about formal diction and illustrate specific stylistic features.
Good character analysis in Austenâs works demonstrates coherent understanding of how diction reflects societal expectations.
Methodology is unclear; detail how you will compare diction (e.g., corpus selection, coding scheme, statistical measures) to strengthen your approach.
The discussion of modernist authors ends abruptly, indicating incomplete editing; ensure each section is fully developed.
The link between social media influence and colloquial diction in modern romance is insightful, highlighting cultural relevance.
Analysis remains at summary level without concrete data or textual metrics; integrate quantifiable evidence to substantiate claims.
The paragraph contains run-on sentences and inconsistent punctuation; break into focused ideas to improve readability.
Use specific excerpts annotated for tone, register, or lexical choice to illustrate modern diction shifts in texts like âTwisted Love.â
Repeated misuse of âaddictionâ for âdictionâ in analysis distracts from your argument; revise for terminological accuracy.
Enhance your comparative discussion by aligning specific narrative parallels with diction features in a structured format.
Character names are inconsistent and sometimes incorrect (âErinâ vs. âAaronâ); verify all details to maintain credibility.
This thematic comparison shows deep insight into emotional restraint vs. expression across periods, reflecting strong critical analysis.
In the conclusion, acknowledge study limitations (e.g., sample size, source scope) and suggest avenues for future research.
Bibliography relies solely on primary texts and displays inconsistent formatting; adopt one citation style and include secondary academic sources.
Include an abstract summarizing the research question, methodology, and key findings to meet structural expectations.
